title: Listening - Part 3: The Ghost Office
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
instructions: You will hear an interview with an urbanist, Thomas, and an economic analyst, Ada, about how remote work is changing our cities. For questions 15–20, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which fits best.

extracts.context: An interview regarding the 'death' of central business districts and the rise of local economic clusters.
extracts.audio_script:
Interviewer: For a century, our cities were built like a wheel, with everyone commuting into the center. Now, that wheel is falling apart. I’m with Thomas, an urban planner, and Ada, an economist. Thomas, are we witnessing the end of the 'city center' as we know it?

Thomas: [15] It’s more of an evolution than an ending. The mono-functional 'Central Business District'—those areas that are hives of activity at noon and ghost towns at 8 PM—is definitely dead. [/15] People no longer accept a two-hour commute just to sit in a cubicle. What we’re seeing is a shift toward 'mixed-use' development. The city center has to become a place where people live, play, and eat, not just where they process spreadsheets. If it doesn't offer an 'experience,' people simply won't come.

Interviewer: Ada, how is this affecting the 'sandwich economy'—the small businesses that relied on office workers?

Ada: [16] It’s been catastrophic for city-center retail, but it’s a 'relocation' of wealth rather than its destruction. That five dollars you used to spend on coffee near your office is now being spent at the bakery in your local suburb. [/16] We’re seeing a 'donut effect' where the center empties out, but the edges become more vibrant. The challenge for governments is that their tax systems are still designed for the old 'hub and spoke' model, leaving them with a massive budget hole in the center.

Interviewer: Thomas, is this good for the environment? Less commuting should mean fewer emissions, right?

Thomas: [17] Paradoxically, it’s not that simple. While daily commutes are down, 'lifestyle travel' is up. People who work from home often move further into the countryside, where they become more car-dependent for every single trip to the supermarket. [/17] We also see 'super-commuters' who fly across the country once a month for meetings. The carbon footprint is shifting rather than disappearing. True sustainability requires 'density'—having everything you need within walking distance, wherever you live.

Interviewer: Ada, what about the impact on young workers? Can you build a career from your bedroom?

Ada: [18] This is my biggest worry. For a junior employee, the office is where 'passive learning' happens. You overhear a senior partner on a phone call, or you have a quick chat at the coffee machine. You can't schedule those moments on a Zoom call. [/18] We risk creating a 'lost generation' of workers who have the technical skills but lack the social capital and the professional network that only physical proximity can provide.

Interviewer: Thomas, some developers are turning old offices into apartments. Is that the solution?

Thomas: [19] It’s harder than it looks. Office buildings have 'deep floor plates'—the middle of the building is far from any window. Turning that into a livable apartment with natural light and plumbing is a nightmare. [/19] It’s often cheaper to knock the building down and start again. But we have to find a way, because we can't just leave millions of square feet of concrete sitting empty while we have a housing crisis.

Interviewer: Final thoughts, Ada? What’s your advice for a city like London or New York?

Ada: [20] Stop trying to 'get back to normal.' The old normal is gone. Instead, focus on 'quality of life.' Make the streets walkable, invest in parks, and make it easier for small, creative businesses to take over those empty storefronts. The cities that survive will be the ones that people *want* to be in, not the ones they *have* to be in. [/20]

Interviewer: Thomas, Ada, thank you both.

questions.question: Thomas believes that for city centers to survive, they must
options: A) reduce the cost of renting office space for large companies.
B) provide reasons for people to visit other than just work.
C) increase the speed and frequency of commuter trains.
D) ban all remote work for people who live within the city limits.
questions.explanation: He says the center must become a place to 'live, play, and eat' and offer an 'experience' because people won't come just for cubicles.

questions.question: What does Ada mean by the 'donut effect'?
options: A) a new type of fast-food business that is popular in suburbs
B) a decrease in the price of food in the city center
C) the shift of economic activity from the center to the outer edges
D) the way tax systems are failing to collect enough money.
questions.explanation: She explains that while the center empties out, the 'edges become more vibrant' as people spend money locally.

questions.question: Thomas argues that remote work might not be better for the environment because
options: A) people are using more electricity in their homes than in offices.
B) remote workers are more likely to move to areas where they must drive more.
C) the delivery of products to homes creates more pollution than shops.
D) people are taking more frequent international holidays.
questions.explanation: He says people moving to the countryside become 'more car-dependent for every single trip' and some fly long distances for occasional meetings.

questions.question: Ada is concerned about young workers because she thinks they will
options: A) miss out on the informal learning that happens in an office.
B) not be able to afford the high cost of home office equipment.
C) find it too easy to hide their mistakes from their managers.
D) refuse to work for companies that require occasional travel.
questions.explanation: She mentions 'passive learning' like overhearing senior partners and chatting at coffee machines which can't be scheduled.

questions.question: Why is it difficult to convert office buildings into homes according to Thomas?
options: A) The buildings are too close to noisy traffic.
B) They are usually located in areas without schools.
C) The architecture makes it hard to provide enough natural light.
D) The owners of the buildings are waiting for offices to return.
questions.explanation: He explains that 'deep floor plates' mean the middle of the building is far from windows, making plumbing and light a 'nightmare'.

questions.question: Ada's final advice to major cities is to
options: A) focus on bringing the large office workers back as soon as possible.
B) prioritize the environment and the happiness of the residents.
C) decrease the amount of park space to build more affordable homes.
D) wait until the economy returns to the way it was in 2019.
questions.explanation: She says to focus on 'quality of life', making streets walkable and investing in parks so people 'want' to be there.
